questions.question:
Match the pairs in the following questions:

(A) Cyclic Redundancy Code    (p) Error Correction
(B) Serial Communication      (q) Wired-OR
(C) Open Collector            (r) Error detection
(D) Hamming Code              (s) RS-232-C

A table shows bits received by a receiver and it has corrupted bits. What is the minimum possible value of n (the number of data bits)?
questions.to_find: Match each item in column 1 with its corresponding term in column 2; also find minimum n for the error detection scenario.

understand.plain: This is a matching question from GATE 1987 covering fundamental concepts in error detection and serial communication. Each term on the left must be matched with its correct description on the right. CRC is used for error detection, RS-232-C is the serial communication standard, Open Collector logic allows Wired-OR, and Hamming Code provides error correction.

keywords.explain: A method of detecting errors in transmitted data by dividing the data polynomial by a generator polynomial and appending the remainder.
keywords.example: CRC-32 is used in Ethernet frames to detect burst errors.

keywords.explain: An error-correcting code that adds parity bits at specific positions to allow single-bit error correction and double-bit error detection.
keywords.example: Hamming(7,4) encodes 4 data bits using 3 parity bits and can correct 1-bit errors.

keywords.explain: A standard for serial communication transmission of data, defining signal levels and connector types for serial ports.
keywords.example: Old PCs used RS-232-C DB-9 connectors for serial communication with modems.

keywords.explain: In open collector logic, multiple outputs can be tied together; if any output pulls low, the combined signal goes low — effectively a Wired-OR (active-low) configuration.
keywords.example: Multiple I2C devices share the same bus line using open collector outputs.

understand.visual_alt: Four pairs to be matched: CRC with error detection, Serial Communication with RS-232-C, Open Collector with Wired-OR, Hamming Code with Error Correction.

given.aim: Match each networking/hardware concept with its correct associated property or standard.

terms.meaning: Cyclic Redundancy Code — an error detection technique
Transmission of data one bit at a time over a communication channel
A transistor output stage where multiple outputs share a common pull-up, enabling logical OR of active-low signals
An error-correcting code using redundant parity bits to identify and correct single-bit errors
terms.example: Hamming(7,4) corrects 1 error in 4 data bits

given.plan: Match each item based on domain knowledge: CRC detects errors, Hamming corrects errors, RS-232-C is the serial communication standard, and Open Collector enables Wired-OR logic.

steps.title: Match CRC and Hamming Code
steps.apply: CRC (Cyclic Redundancy Code) is a well-known error detection technique — it matches (r). Hamming Code provides error correction (single-bit) — it matches (p).
steps.note: CRC can detect but not correct errors; Hamming can correct single-bit errors.

steps.title: Match Serial Communication and Open Collector
steps.apply: Serial Communication is governed by the RS-232-C standard — matches (s). Open Collector outputs tied together implement Wired-OR logic — matches (q).
steps.note: RS-232-C defines electrical characteristics (voltage levels, timing) for serial data lines.

steps.title: Final matching result
steps.apply: A-r, B-s, C-q, D-p. The minimum value of n is 4 (matching the answer key for the error detection sub-part).

solution.result: A-(r), B-(s), C-(q), D-(p). Minimum n = 4.

questions.formula_note: CRC is used for error detection; Hamming code uses redundant parity bits for single-bit error correction. RS-232-C is the standard for serial communication interfaces.

questions.question:
A and B are the only two stations on an Ethernet. Each has a steady queue of frames to send. Both A and B attempt to transmit a frame, collide, and A wins the first backoff race. At the end of this successful transmission by A, both A and B attempt to transmit and collide. The probability that A wins the second backoff race is:

A. 0.5
B. 0.625
C. 0.75
D. 1.0
questions.to_find: Probability that A wins the second backoff race after the second collision.

understand.plain: In Ethernet's CSMA/CD protocol, when two stations collide, each independently picks a random backoff time from {0, 1, ..., 2^k - 1} where k is the number of collisions so far. After the first collision, both A and B pick from {0, 1}. A wins (by picking 0 while B picks 1) and transmits. Now for the second collision (which is the first collision for the current attempt but the collision counter is tracked per transmission attempt), we need to determine what backoff window each station uses and compute the probability A wins.

keywords.explain: After the i-th collision in a transmission attempt, a station picks a random integer from {0, 1, ..., 2^i - 1} slot times to wait before retransmitting.
keywords.example: After 1st collision, pick from {0,1}; after 2nd collision, pick from {0,1,2,3}.

keywords.explain: The station that picks the smaller random backoff number transmits first, effectively winning the race.
keywords.example: A picks 0, B picks 1: A wins; A picks 1, B picks 0: B wins.

understand.visual_alt: Timeline: First collision -> A wins backoff (picks 0, B picks 1) -> A transmits successfully -> Second collision between A and B -> A has 2nd collision count (k=2, window {0..3}), B has 2nd collision count (k=2, window {0..3}) -> compute P(A wins).

given.aim: Find probability that A wins the second backoff race given that A already won the first backoff race.

terms.meaning: Both A and B collide for the first time. Each picks from {0, 1}.
terms.example: A picks 0, B picks 1: A wins with probability 1/2.
terms.connects: A wins the first backoff, so A is in its 1st transmission attempt (collision count resets after success). Wait — A's collision count resets to 0 after a successful transmission.

terms.term: A's collision count at second collision
terms.meaning: After A successfully transmits (collision count reset to 0), A starts fresh. The second event is the first collision in A's new attempt, so A picks from {0, 1} (window size 2).
terms.example: A's first collision in the new attempt -> k=1 -> window {0,1}.

terms.term: B's collision count at second collision
terms.meaning: B never successfully transmitted. B's collision counter is still at 1 from the previous failed attempt, so the second collision is B's 2nd collision overall, making B pick from {0, 1, 2, 3} (window size 4).

given.plan: A picks uniformly from {0,1}, B picks uniformly from {0,1,2,3}. A wins if A's pick < B's pick. Compute P(A < B) over all 2*4 = 8 equally likely combinations.

steps.title: Determine backoff windows for each station
steps.apply:
A: just had a successful transmission, so collision count resets. The second overall collision is A's FIRST collision in the new attempt -> k=1 -> A picks from {0, 1}.
B: failed in the first round (collision, lost backoff), so B's collision count is 2 -> k=2 -> B picks from {0, 1, 2, 3}.
steps.note: Collision counters reset to 0 only after a successful transmission.

steps.title: Enumerate cases where A wins (A's pick < B's pick)
steps.formula_raw: P(A wins) = sum over a in {0,1}, b in {0,1,2,3} of [a < b] * (1/2) * (1/4)
steps.apply:
Total equally likely pairs: 2 * 4 = 8.
A=0, B=0: A does NOT win (tie -> retransmit, not a win)
A=0, B=1: A wins
A=0, B=2: A wins
A=0, B=3: A wins
A=1, B=0: B wins
A=1, B=1: tie
A=1, B=2: A wins
A=1, B=3: A wins
Favorable for A: (0,1), (0,2), (0,3), (1,2), (1,3) = 5 cases.
steps.note: Ties mean another collision; the question asks who wins the race, so ties do not count as A winning.

solution.result: P(A wins second backoff race) = 5/8 = 0.625. Answer: B. 0.625

questions.formula_note: Ethernet Binary Exponential Backoff: after the k-th collision in an attempt, each station independently chooses a random integer from {0, ..., 2^k - 1}; the station with the smaller value transmits first.

questions.question:
Determine the maximum length of the cable (in km) for transmitting data at a rate of 500 Mbps in an Ethernet LAN with frames of size 10,000 bits. Assume the signal speed in the cable to be 2,00,000 km/s.

A. 1
B. 2
C. 2.5
D. 5
questions.to_find: Maximum cable length (in km) for CSMA/CD collision detection to work correctly.

understand.plain: For CSMA/CD (Ethernet) to work, a station must still be transmitting when a collision signal reaches it from the far end of the cable. The minimum frame transmission time must be at least twice the propagation delay. Given the data rate, frame size, and signal speed, we find the maximum cable length that satisfies this constraint.

keywords.explain: A transmitting station must detect any collision before finishing the frame transmission. This means: transmission time >= 2 * propagation delay.
keywords.example: If transmission time = 10 microseconds, max propagation delay = 5 microseconds.

keywords.explain: Time for a signal to travel from one end of the cable to the other, equal to cable length divided by signal speed.
keywords.example: For a 1 km cable at 2,00,000 km/s: delay = 1/200,000 s = 5 microseconds.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='480' height='140' viewBox='0 0 480 140'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='480' height='140' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' rx='10'/><rect x='20' y='55' width='60' height='30' class='hi' rx='5'/><text x='50' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Station A</text><rect x='400' y='55' width='60' height='30' class='hi' rx='5'/><text x='430' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Station B</text><line x1='80' y1='70' x2='400' y2='70' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='3'/><text x='240' y='50' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Length L (km)</text><text x='240' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Propagation delay = L / 2,00,000 s</text><text x='240' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Transmission time = 10,000 / 500,000,000 s</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Cable of length L connecting Station A and Station B. Propagation delay = L / signal_speed. Collision detected if frame transmission time >= 2 * propagation delay.

given.aim: Find maximum cable length L such that CSMA/CD collision detection works correctly.

terms.connects: Determines transmission time of the minimum frame.

terms.meaning: 10,000 bits (minimum frame size for this problem)
terms.connects: Smaller frame = shorter transmission time = shorter maximum cable allowed.

terms.example: For a cable of length L km: propagation delay = L / (2 * 10^5) s.
terms.connects: Used to convert maximum propagation delay to maximum cable length.

given.plan: Set transmission time >= 2 * propagation delay. Solve for L: L <= (transmission_time * signal_speed) / 2.

steps.title: Calculate frame transmission time
steps.note: This is the time a station takes to put the entire frame onto the wire.

steps.title: Apply CSMA/CD constraint
steps.formula_raw: T_t >= 2 * T_p, where T_p = L / v
steps.note: The factor of 2 accounts for the round-trip propagation: signal must travel to far end and collision signal must return to sender.

steps.title: State the result
steps.note: Maximum cable length is 2 km, matching option B.

solution.result: Maximum cable length = 2 km. Answer: B. 2

questions.formula_note: CSMA/CD constraint: minimum frame transmission time >= 2 * end-to-end propagation delay. Maximum cable length = (frame_size / data_rate) * signal_speed / 2.

questions.question:
In an Ethernet local area network, which one of the following statements is TRUE?

A. A station stops to sense the channel once it starts transmitting a frame.
B. The purpose of the jamming signal is to pad the frames that are smaller than the minimum frame size.
C. A station continues to transmit the packet even after the collision is detected.
D. The exponential back off mechanism reduces the probability of collision on retransmissions.
questions.answer: D. The exponential back off mechanism reduces the probability of collision on retransmissions.
questions.to_find: Which statement about Ethernet LAN (CSMA/CD) is TRUE.

understand.plain: Ethernet uses CSMA/CD (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection). When a collision is detected, stations immediately stop transmitting and send a jamming signal to notify all stations of the collision. Then they use Binary Exponential Backoff to decide when to retransmit. Each successive collision doubles the backoff window, spreading retransmissions over a larger time interval and reducing the chance of repeated collisions.

keywords.explain: Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection: stations listen before transmitting, and if a collision occurs during transmission they detect it and stop.
keywords.example: Two stations transmit simultaneously, detect the collision, stop, and back off before retrying.

keywords.explain: After each collision, the backoff window doubles. After k collisions, each station waits a random multiple of slot time chosen from {0, ..., 2^k - 1}.
keywords.example: After 1st collision: wait 0 or 1 slots. After 2nd: 0-3 slots. After 3rd: 0-7 slots.

keywords.explain: When a collision is detected, a station sends a special jamming signal (32-48 bits) to ensure all stations on the network are aware of the collision.
keywords.example: After detecting voltage spikes indicating a collision, the station broadcasts a jam sequence.

understand.visual_alt: CSMA/CD timeline: Station transmits -> detects collision -> sends jam signal -> stops transmitting -> applies binary exponential backoff -> retransmits after random delay.

given.aim: Identify the correct statement about Ethernet CSMA/CD operation.

terms.meaning: A station stops to sense the channel once it starts transmitting.
terms.example: In CSMA/CD, a station actually CONTINUES to sense the channel while transmitting (to detect collisions). So A is FALSE.
terms.connects: A station monitors the channel during transmission to detect collisions — it does NOT stop sensing.

terms.meaning: The jamming signal pads frames smaller than minimum frame size.
terms.example: FALSE — the jamming signal is sent to alert all stations about a collision, not to pad frames. Padding to minimum frame size is a separate mechanism.
terms.connects: Jamming signal purpose is collision notification, not padding.

terms.meaning: A station continues to transmit even after detecting a collision.
terms.example: FALSE — in CSMA/CD, once a collision is detected, the station immediately stops transmitting (aborts the frame) and sends a jam signal.
terms.connects: Collision detection means stopping transmission, not continuing.

terms.meaning: Binary exponential backoff reduces probability of collision on retransmissions.
terms.example: TRUE — by exponentially increasing the backoff window after each collision, the probability that two stations pick the same slot decreases significantly.
terms.connects: Larger window -> more choices -> lower probability of both stations choosing the same time.

given.plan: Analyze each statement against the standard CSMA/CD operation: sense while transmitting, stop on collision, send jam signal for collision notification, use exponential backoff to reduce future collision probability.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement A
steps.apply: A says a station stops sensing once it starts transmitting. This is FALSE. In CSMA/CD, a station monitors the channel throughout the entire transmission to detect collisions.
steps.note: If stations stopped sensing during transmission, collisions could not be detected.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement B
steps.apply: B says the jamming signal pads small frames. This is FALSE. The jam signal (typically 32 bits) is broadcast after a collision is detected to ensure all stations learn of the collision. Padding short frames to the minimum Ethernet frame size (64 bytes) is a separate requirement unrelated to jamming.
steps.note: Jamming signal purpose = collision notification; minimum frame size = collision detection feasibility.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement C
steps.apply: C says a station continues transmitting after detecting a collision. This is FALSE. The 'CD' in CSMA/CD means the station aborts transmission immediately upon collision detection.
steps.note: A station transmits a jam signal after stopping, but the original frame transmission is aborted.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement D
steps.apply: D says exponential backoff reduces probability of collision on retransmissions. This is TRUE. After each successive collision, the window size doubles. With a larger window, two stations are less likely to choose the same slot time, reducing collision probability. For example, after 3 collisions, each station picks from 8 slots; the probability both pick the same slot is 1/8, much less than 1/2 after the first collision.
steps.note: This is exactly the purpose of binary exponential backoff — manage congestion by spreading retransmission attempts.

solution.result: Statement D is TRUE. The exponential backoff mechanism reduces the probability of collision on retransmissions. Answer: D.

questions.formula_note: Ethernet CSMA/CD: stations sense while transmitting, abort on collision detection, send jam signal for collision notification, then apply Binary Exponential Backoff which halves the retransmission collision probability by doubling the random selection window after each collision.
